chunk_text:
IMPACT REPORT Sustainable Solutions Lab Pursuing Climate Justice UMB.EDU/SSL We are grateful to be a part of a community of researchers, practitioners, and
organizations pushing to create a just and resilient world for all. Thank you to
our partners and funders for their continued commitment to this work. Partner Institutes at University of Massachusetts, Boston
The Institute for Asian American Studies
The Institute for New England Native American Studies
The Mauricio Gastón Institute for Latino Community
Development and Public Policy
The William Monroe Trotter Institute for the Study
of Black History and Culture Our Team
What We Do: Advancing Climate Justice
Connecting and Convening Visiting Scholars

chunk_text:
The Sustainable Solutions Lab (SSL) at the 
(UMB) is a collaborative research and action institute focused on keeping
historically excluded people and communities safe and healthy in the face
of climate change. What We Do:
Advancing Climate Justice
Northeast Climate Justice Research Collaborative
Launched in Spring 2022, the Northeast Climate Justice Research Collaborative
(the Collaborative) is a transdisciplinary network that brings together academic and
community researchers to advance climate practices and policies that prioritize
the needs of historically excluded communities.
embedding_text:
The Sustainable Solutions Lab (SSL) at the 
(UMB) is a collaborative research and action institute focused on keeping
historically excluded people and communities safe and healthy in the face
of climate change. What We Do:
Advancing Climate Justice
Northeast Climate Justice Research Collaborative
Launched in Spring 2022, the Northeast Climate Justice Research Collaborative
(the Collaborative) is a transdisciplinary network that brings together academic and
community researchers to advance climate practices and policies that prioritize
the needs of historically excluded communities.

chunk_text:
What We Do:
Advancing Climate Justice
Northeast Climate Justice Research Collaborative
Launched in Spring 2022, the Northeast Climate Justice Research Collaborative
(the Collaborative) is a transdisciplinary network that brings together academic and
community researchers to advance climate practices and policies that prioritize
the needs of historically excluded communities. By fostering connections through
networking, dialogue, and resource sharing, the Collaborative empowers researchers
to maximize the impact of their work on Environmental Justice communities. In 2023, we hosted the frst in-person workshop for Collaborative members on
Transdisciplinary Climate Justice Research.
embedding_text:
What We Do:
Advancing Climate Justice
Northeast Climate Justice Research Collaborative
Launched in Spring 2022, the Northeast Climate Justice Research Collaborative
(the Collaborative) is a transdisciplinary network that brings together academic and
community researchers to advance climate practices and policies that prioritize
the needs of historically excluded communities. By fostering connections through
networking, dialogue, and resource sharing, the Collaborative empowers researchers
to maximize the impact of their work on Environmental Justice communities. In 2023, we hosted the frst in-person workshop for Collaborative members on
Transdisciplinary Climate Justice Research.

chunk_text:
By fostering connections through
networking, dialogue, and resource sharing, the Collaborative empowers researchers
to maximize the impact of their work on Environmental Justice communities. In 2023, we hosted the frst in-person workshop for Collaborative members on
Transdisciplinary Climate Justice Research. In 2024, we hosted an in-person
workshop on Transformative Community Engagement, in partnership with the
Massachusetts Ecosystem Climate Adaptation Network (Mass ECAN) at the
University of Massachusetts Amherst.

In 2023, we hosted the frst in-person workshop for Collaborative members on
Transdisciplinary Climate Justice Research. In 2024, we hosted an in-person
workshop on Transformative Community Engagement, in partnership with the
Massachusetts Ecosystem Climate Adaptation Network (Mass ECAN) at the
University of Massachusetts Amherst. We also hosted 11 Collaborative Friday
presentations, featuring insightful and boundary-pushing presentations like:
Connecting and Convening
New leadership and strategic directions
In December 2023, we said goodbye to longtime SSL
Director, Rebecca Herst.

New leadership and strategic directions
In December 2023, we said goodbye to longtime SSL
Director, Rebecca Herst. Rebecca was instrumental in
shaping SSL into what it is today —building bridges and
relationships, centering racial and climate justice, and
promoting resilience and adaptation at UMB and in the
region.

New leadership and strategic directions
In December 2023, we said goodbye to longtime SSL
Director, Rebecca Herst. Rebecca was instrumental in
shaping SSL into what it is today —building bridges and
relationships, centering racial and climate justice, and
promoting resilience and adaptation at UMB and in the
region. We are forever grateful for her leadership and vision. In February 2024, SSL welcomed our new Executive Director,
Dr. B.R.

chunk_text:
In February 2024, SSL welcomed our new Executive Director,
Dr. B.R. Balachandran, who has since made great strides in
expanding funding and programs for SSL and shepherding us
into a new chapter. He came to SSL with signifcant experience and scholarship
in urban and regional planning focused on post-disaster recovery, disaster
mitigation, and sustainability. Before becoming SSL’s ED, he held a visiting faculty
position at UMB’s School for the Environment and a research position at the
University of North Texas at Denton (UNT).
embedding_text:
In February 2024, SSL welcomed our new Executive Director,
Dr. B.R. Balachandran, who has since made great strides in
expanding funding and programs for SSL and shepherding us
into a new chapter. He came to SSL with signifcant experience and scholarship
in urban and regional planning focused on post-disaster recovery, disaster
mitigation, and sustainability. Before becoming SSL’s ED, he held a visiting faculty
position at UMB’s School for the Environment and a research position at the
University of North Texas at Denton (UNT).

chunk_text:
He came to SSL with signifcant experience and scholarship
in urban and regional planning focused on post-disaster recovery, disaster
mitigation, and sustainability. Before becoming SSL’s ED, he held a visiting faculty
position at UMB’s School for the Environment and a research position at the
University of North Texas at Denton (UNT). In 2018, with generous support from the Barr Foundation, SSL underwent
an in-depth multi-stakeholder Strategic Planning efort, which set up crucial
frameworks, priorities, and governance.

chunk_text:
Before becoming SSL’s ED, he held a visiting faculty
position at UMB’s School for the Environment and a research position at the
University of North Texas at Denton (UNT). In 2018, with generous support from the Barr Foundation, SSL underwent
an in-depth multi-stakeholder Strategic Planning efort, which set up crucial
frameworks, priorities, and governance. Since then, there have been several
smaller-scale processes, the most recent of which was in 2024, which helped SSL
identify strategic and governance priorities for the next 5 years.

May 2, 2025
“
It's not a coincidence that I’ve come to nearly every
Climate Adaptation Forum since it was created and am
now Cambridge’s Chief Climate Officer. This has been
a six-year graduate-level practicum for all of us.

chunk_text:
This has been
a six-year graduate-level practicum for all of us. I also don’t
think it’s a coincidence that Boston hasn’t experienced
a backlash against the planning and investments we’re
making, even though we haven’t had a big disaster. This
forum has been moving us all along together. Climate Adaptation Forum
The Climate Adaptation Forum (CAF) is a quarterly series of half-day events
organized by the Environmental Business Council of New England and SSL. Since 2017, the CAF has been a regular, ongoing opportunity for adaptation
professionals and practitioners from various fields to hear from local, national,
and global thought leaders, network, and share solutions to common problems.

The Climate Adaptation Forum (CAF) is a quarterly series of half-day events
organized by the Environmental Business Council of New England and SSL. Since 2017, the CAF has been a regular, ongoing opportunity for adaptation
professionals and practitioners from various fields to hear from local, national,

Connecting and Convening (cont.)
Climate Justice Deep Dives
In Fall 2023, SSL hosted its second series of
Climate Justice Deep Dives.

Climate Justice Deep Dives
In Fall 2023, SSL hosted its second series of
Climate Justice Deep Dives. This semester-long
program provided a collaborative learning space for
UMass Boston scholars to explore the intersections
of climate change, inequality, and systemic racism.

chunk_text:
Recent events include:
Connecting and Convening (cont.)
Climate Justice Deep Dives
In Fall 2023, SSL hosted its second series of
Climate Justice Deep Dives. This semester-long
program provided a collaborative learning space for
UMass Boston scholars to explore the intersections
of climate change, inequality, and systemic racism. Participants engaged in discussions on current trends,
identified pressing research needs, and fostered
meaningful connections across disciplines.

In October 2024, SSL hosted the launch
of Climate Justice and Public Health,
a groundbreaking book co-edited by Rajini
Srikanth, Dean of Faculty at UMass Boston,
and Linda Thompson, President of Westfield
State University.

chunk_text:
Participants engaged in discussions on current trends,
identified pressing research needs, and fostered
meaningful connections across disciplines. Book Launch
In October 2024, SSL hosted the launch
of Climate Justice and Public Health,
a groundbreaking book co-edited by Rajini
Srikanth, Dean of Faculty at UMass Boston,
and Linda Thompson, President of Westfield
State University. Featuring contributions from
over 50 authors across diverse fields, the book
addresses critical topics such as Indigenous
health, mental and emotional well-being, senior
health, and the disproportionate impacts of climate
change on African American communities.

chunk_text:
Book Launch
In October 2024, SSL hosted the launch
of Climate Justice and Public Health,
a groundbreaking book co-edited by Rajini
Srikanth, Dean of Faculty at UMass Boston,
and Linda Thompson, President of Westfield
State University. Featuring contributions from
over 50 authors across diverse fields, the book
addresses critical topics such as Indigenous
health, mental and emotional well-being, senior
health, and the disproportionate impacts of climate
change on African American communities. This book
is an example of advancing interdisciplinary, innovative,
and holistic approaches to address health disparities
intensified by climate change.

chunk_text:
Improving Water Quality with NEEFC,
INENAS and Gastón
SSL is a sub-awardee and partner of the New England Environmental
Finance Center (NEEFC) as part of the New England Water Innovation
Network (NEWIN). This partnership focuses on improving access to
funding and technical support for underserved communities addressing
water infrastructure challenges. SSL’s team includes Dr. Cedric Woods,
Dr. B.R. Cedric Woods,
Dr. B.R. Balachandran, Dr. Fabián Torres-Ardila, Dr. Elizabeth (Betsy)
Sweet, and Dr.

chunk_text:
Mattapan Food and Fitness Coalition (MFFC)
The Mattapan Food and Fitness Coalition (MFFC) focuses on food
access and nutrition education, physical activity, youth development,
built environment, and community empowerment in Mattapan. Together
with SSL, MFFC hosted a community listening session with Mattapan,
Dorchester, and Roxbury residents and organizational leaders to better
understand how climate change impacts their lives, what projects and
topics are most important to them, and what role a research institute
like SSL and its CLIIR Initiative have in helping to address these.

chunk_text:
Ofce of Campus Planning and Sustainability,
UMB
SSL collaborated with the Ofce of Campus Planning and Sustainability
to design and launch an innovative Honors College course titled
"Unraveling Sustainability: Can We Have It All?" The course invites
students to step into the role of "sustainability detectives," analyzing
real-world cases on campus and beyond. Ofered in Spring 2025,
this course provides an interdisciplinary exploration of sustainability
challenges and opportunities, tailored specifcally for students in the
UMass Boston Honors College.

chunk_text:
Cape Cod Rail Resilience Research
SSL is collaborating with the Massachusetts
Department of Transportation (MassDOT) in the
Cape Cod Rail Resilience Research Project, led
by Carlos Velásquez, a PhD candidate at UMass
Boston and project manager at MassDOT. This initiative aims to improve rail safety and
climate resilience along the Cape Main Line
by integrating advanced technology with
stakeholder-engaged research methods. The project, inspired by Velásquez’s doctoral
research, addresses the increasing risks posed
by climate change to critical transportation
infrastructure.
embedding_text:
Cape Cod Rail Resilience Research
SSL is collaborating with the Massachusetts
Department of Transportation (MassDOT) in the
Cape Cod Rail Resilience Research Project, led
by Carlos Velásquez, a PhD candidate at UMass
Boston and project manager at MassDOT. This initiative aims to improve rail safety and
climate resilience along the Cape Main Line
by integrating advanced technology with
stakeholder-engaged research methods. The project, inspired by Velásquez’s doctoral
research, addresses the increasing risks posed
by climate change to critical transportation
infrastructure.

chunk_text:
This initiative aims to improve rail safety and
climate resilience along the Cape Main Line
by integrating advanced technology with
stakeholder-engaged research methods. The project, inspired by Velásquez’s doctoral
research, addresses the increasing risks posed
by climate change to critical transportation
infrastructure. It was launched in response to a
signifcant 300-foot rail embankment collapse in
East Sandwich in 2020, an event linked to climate
change-induced drought conditions. With USDOT
funding, the team is using drones for mapping and
sensors for monitoring water levels. This collaborative
efort brings together MassDOT, UMass Boston, and AECOM
experts.

chunk_text:
With USDOT
funding, the team is using drones for mapping and
sensors for monitoring water levels. This collaborative
efort brings together MassDOT, UMass Boston, and AECOM
experts. The innovative approach combines aerial surveys, hydrological drought
analysis, and real-time monitoring systems at three pilot study sites. C3 Initiative

chunk_text:
C3 Initiative
The Climate Careers Curricula Initiative (C3 Initiative), supported by the Liberty
Mutual Foundation (LMF), aims to create and ofer micro-credentialed training
programs for blue and green jobs in Greater Boston. Its goal is to collaborate
with industry partners, community-based organizations, and academic experts to
develop training programs for high-demand blue and green jobs, providing career
pathways for underrepresented populations, especially vulnerable young adults,
people of color, and low-income adults from environmental justice communities.

chunk_text:
Strengthening Indigenous Resilience: A Community-
Led Approach to Climate Adaptation for the Lumbee
Tribe of North Carolina
The Lumbee Tribe of North Carolina (LTNC) has launched an initiative to address
the Tribe’s disaster resilience needs in collaboration with UMB. Located in foodprone Robeson County, the tribe faces repeated impacts from hurricanes and
limited access to federal disaster resources. The project will document LTNC’s
disaster response experiences during Hurricanes Matthew, Florence, and
Debby, focusing on responses by their staf, resource mobilization, and resilience
practices.
embedding_text:
Strengthening Indigenous Resilience: A Community-
Led Approach to Climate Adaptation for the Lumbee
Tribe of North Carolina
The Lumbee Tribe of North Carolina (LTNC) has launched an initiative to address
the Tribe’s disaster resilience needs in collaboration with UMB. Located in foodprone Robeson County, the tribe faces repeated impacts from hurricanes and
limited access to federal disaster resources. The project will document LTNC’s
disaster response experiences during Hurricanes Matthew, Florence, and
Debby, focusing on responses by their staf, resource mobilization, and resilience
practices.

chunk_text:
Located in foodprone Robeson County, the tribe faces repeated impacts from hurricanes and
limited access to federal disaster resources. The project will document LTNC’s
disaster response experiences during Hurricanes Matthew, Florence, and
Debby, focusing on responses by their staf, resource mobilization, and resilience
practices. This community-led participatory research and action initiative is led
by Kevin Melvin (Tribal Historic Preservation Ofce, Agriculture and Natural
Resources Department, LTNC), in collaboration with Cedric Woods (Director of
the Institute for New England Native American Studies and LTNC member),
and B.R. Balachandran (Executive Director, SSL).

chunk_text:
Visiting Scholars
In 2023-2024, SSL had the privilege of hosting two visiting scholars to further
our commitment to collaboration and climate resilience research. Each scholar
contributed their own expertise and perspective to our work in coastal governance,
risk management, and social resilience. Cassandre Rey-Thibault
(Summer 2024)
Cassandre is a postdoctoral researcher at the Centre
for European Studies and Comparative Politics (CEE)
at Sciences Po Paris, funded by the Bruno Latour Fund. With a PhD in geography, her research focuses on risk
prevention and crisis management policies in French coastal
urban areas.

prevention and crisis management policies in French coastal

chunk_text:
Cassandre Rey-Thibault
(Summer 2024)
Cassandre is a postdoctoral researcher at the Centre
for European Studies and Comparative Politics (CEE)
at Sciences Po Paris, funded by the Bruno Latour Fund. With a PhD in geography, her research focuses on risk
prevention and crisis management policies in French coastal
urban areas. During her time at SSL, Cassandre conducted
research interviews and explored the evolution of risk
governance in the Boston metropolitan area, supporting
SSL’s eforts in collaborative climate resilience research.

aims to enhance public policy on coastal risk management

coastal zone management, holding a PhD from

and climate adaptation. At SSL, Ante contributed his
